humanities_role_explanation: Uses cultural heritage artifacts from museums as domain content while being designed specifically for cultural metadata inference tasks including identifying creators, periods, origins, and cultural contexts
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Multimodal benchmark where VLMs process heritage artifact images and generate structured cultural metadata predictions (culture, period, origin, creator) which are then evaluated using an LLM-as-Judge framework
analytical_tasks_explanation: Tasks require inferring non-observable cultural attributes from visual input including cultural classification (e.g., Chinese, Greek), historical period dating (e.g., Tang dynasty, 5th century BCE), geographical origin identification, and creator attribution - all requiring cultural knowledge beyond visual perception

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Cross-cultural benchmark evaluating multiple VLM architectures on structured cultural metadata inference across diverse cultural contexts

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates 9 VLMs including Qwen-VL series, Pixtral-12B, GPT-4.1-mini, GPT-5.4-mini, and Claude Haiku 4.5

llm_is_primary_system.note: VLMs are the primary systems being benchmarked for cultural metadata inference capabilities

arts_humanities_data.note: Visual Arts - Uses cultural heritage artifacts from Getty and Metropolitan Museum collections across ceramics, paintings, metalwork, and sculpture

quantitative_metrics.note: Reports exact match accuracy, partial match rates, and attribute-level accuracy using LLM-as-Judge evaluation framework

published_after_march_2023.note: Published April 8, 2026 according to arXiv submission date

english_language.note: Paper written in English, benchmark uses English metadata

summary: Appear2Meaning introduces a cross-cultural benchmark for evaluating vision-language models' ability to infer structured cultural metadata (creator, origin, period, culture) from heritage artifact images alone. The benchmark includes 750 objects across four cultural regions and four object categories, revealing that while models can capture partial cultural signals, they struggle with complete metadata inference and exhibit substantial performance variation across cultures.
key_findings: All evaluated VLMs achieved very low exact match accuracy (0.005-0.029) but higher partial match rates (0.453-0.658). Performance varied significantly across cultural regions, with East Asia showing highest accuracy and Americas showing lowest. Models performed better on title and creator attributes while struggling with culture, period, and origin inference.
